role_summary: First chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (April 1960 - October 1961, founded at Shaw University in Raleigh); civil rights organizer in Nashville and Atlanta; later four-term mayor of Washington, D.C. (1979-1991, 1995-1999).
ai_reading: Barry is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee, so the corpus reads him only through the voices that name him, and those voices split his life almost exactly along the SNCC / Washington seam. The four corpus interviewees who discuss him at any length divide into two clusters: Lonnie C. King (entry 80) and the Nashville-orbit figures place him at the 1960 founding as the contested first chairman, while Dion Diamond (entry 27) and Timothy Jenkins (entry 120) read him forward through the District of Columbia, where Jenkins calls him "the big magnet" who drew SNCC veterans into city government. The cultural record foregrounds his four-term mayoralty over his eighteen-month SNCC chairmanship, but the cosine geometry here is the reverse: every passage that names him sits inside SNCC-founding and Nashville-movement testimony, never inside a Washington-politics cluster, because the people who recorded oral histories knew him as a movement peer first. His standalone catalog page anchors the SNCC chairmanship lineage (Barry, then Charles F. McDew at entry 16, then John Lewis, Stokely Carmichael, H. Rap Brown, and Phil Hutchings at entry 98); the McDew page documents the Barry-to-McDew handoff directly, drawing on the same Timothy Jenkins passage.
biographical_paragraph: Barry was born March 6, 1936 in Itta Bena, Mississippi to sharecropping parents who relocated to Memphis when he was eight. He completed a B.S. at LeMoyne College and a master's in chemistry at Fisk; while in graduate study at Fisk in 1960 he was elected the first national chairman of SNCC at the organization's founding conference at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina. He served until October 1961, when Charles F. McDew succeeded him. He moved to Washington, D.C. in 1965 to direct SNCC's regional office there and remained in the city for the rest of his life; he was elected to the D.C. school board in 1971, the city council in 1974, and the mayoralty in 1978. He died November 23, 2014 in Washington at seventy-eight.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lonnie C. King
interview_snippets.lead_in: Lonnie C. King, co-founder of the Atlanta Student Movement, recalls the contested selection of the first SNCC chairman, when the Nashville delegation put Barry forward over Diane Nash:
interview_snippets.quote: My name got put up as the first chairman. The folks of Nashville brought in a guy, Marion Barry. That never heard of before as their candidate. I thought it would have been Diane Nash, but it was Barry.

interview_snippets.speaker: Timothy Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: Timothy Jenkins, the SNCC strategist and former National Student Association vice president, recalls a letter Barry sent him during Barry's student years:
interview_snippets.quote: Marion Barry interestingly enough wrote me a four-page letter when he was still a student in which he outlined the importance of blacks taking over the political structures in the localities wherever they lived.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dion Diamond
interview_snippets.lead_in: Dion Diamond, a founding member of Howard University's Nonviolent Action Group and a 1961 Freedom Rider, accounts for the concentration of SNCC veterans in Washington, D.C.:
interview_snippets.quote: Marion Barry is the reason so many of us are here. If you recall, this used to be called Chocolate City. And Marion, when he was mayor, bought more college graduates and SNCC people who ended up in professional positions in city government.

interview_snippets.speaker: Carrie Lamar Young
interview_snippets.lead_in: Arkansas voter-registration organizer Carrie Lamar Young places Barry among the SNCC figures who moved into electoral office:
interview_snippets.quote: Julian Bond went into politics and Andrew Young went into politics, Marion Barry went into politics. And it's, you know, again, it's like the vision was lost with them.

sources.title: Marion Barry Jr. with Omar Tyree, Mayor for Life: The Incredible Story of Marion Barry, Jr.
